column3: User name, email, hashed password, login timestamps, last active, OAuth tokens (if SSO), plan tier
column5: User identity verification, session management, access control

column1: Product Database (PostgreSQL / MySQL / MongoDB)
column3: User ID, email, organisation name, subscription plan, feature usage metadata, prompt counts, API key hashes, billing tier, account creation date
column5: Product functionality, account management, subscription state, billing integration
column7: Collected at registration and updated through product usage
column9: AWS RDS / GCP Cloud SQL / MongoDB Atlas (infrastructure processor)
column12: AES-256 encryption at rest, TLS in transit, RBAC, IAM access controls, VPC isolation
column14: Automated deletion via account deletion API. Verify cascade to all related tables.
column17: Confirm database region. Use Mumbai (AWS ap-south-1) or equivalent to avoid cross-border transfer. Restrict database access to 2–3 engineers maximum. Review access logs monthly.

column1: Third-Party AI API — Prompt Inputs (OpenAI / Anthropic / Google / AWS Bedrock)
column3: User prompt text (may contain personal data entered by user or about third parties), conversation context, system prompts, model parameters
column5: AI model inference — generating product outputs from user inputs
column7: Generated by users during product use — sent to AI API in real time
column8: Engineering team (API configuration only — not stored by default)
column9: OpenAI LLC / Anthropic PBC / Google LLC / Amazon Web Services
column12: TLS in transit, API key authentication, prompt data not used for training (verify and opt out if needed)
column13: Not stored by API provider beyond inference (verify per provider). Inference logs stored separately — see next row.
column14: Prompts not retained by API after inference (verify). Request deletion from provider if in doubt.
column17: CRITICAL: Download and sign DPA from every AI API provider you use. Verify that API inputs are not used for model training (opt out if default is opt-in). Disclose provider name and data destination in your privacy policy. Update if you switch providers.

column1: Inference Logs (internal log store — CloudWatch / Datadog / custom)
column3: User ID, session ID, timestamp, prompt text (may contain personal data), model response text, latency metrics, error codes, token counts
column5: Debugging, product monitoring, abuse detection, quality review
column7: Generated automatically by application during model inference
column9: AWS CloudWatch / Datadog Inc. / custom logging infrastructure
column12: IAM access controls, log encryption at rest, limited retention policy
column13: 30–90 days maximum recommended. Delete automatically on expiry.
column14: Automated log expiry policy. Manual deletion pipeline on user account deletion or erasure request.
column17: HIGHEST RISK ROW. Inference logs often contain user personal data in plain text. Set mandatory retention limits. Implement log redaction for obvious PII (email addresses, phone numbers) before storage. Ensure logs are deleted when user account is deleted. Restrict access to engineering lead only.

column1: Embedding Vector Store (Pinecone / Weaviate / pgvector)
column3: Embedding vectors generated from user documents or prompts, associated metadata (user ID, document ID, chunk ID, source filename)
column5: Semantic search, Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), context retrieval
column7: Generated from user-uploaded documents or conversation history
column9: Pinecone Inc. / Weaviate B.V. / same DB as product (pgvector)
column12: TLS in transit, API key access, namespace isolation per user
column13: Account lifetime. Delete on account deletion or erasure request.
column14: Delete by user namespace or user ID filter. Must be included in account deletion pipeline.
column17: Embedding vectors derived from personal data may themselves be personal data under a cautious interpretation. Treat as personal data. Ensure deletion pipeline covers vector store. Disclose vector storage in privacy policy.

column1: Training Data Store (if fine-tuned model used)
column3: Text samples used to fine-tune model — may contain user-generated content, third-party datasets, or proprietary data. May contain personal data if sourced from user inputs.
column4: Users who generated the training data (if applicable)
column6: Consent (Section 6) — only if user inputs used without separate consent
column7: Assembled by engineering team from product usage data, public datasets, or licensed sources
column9: AWS S3 / GCP Storage / Azure Blob (infrastructure)
column12: Bucket encryption, IAM access controls, no public access
column13: Duration of model usage. Review when model is deprecated.
column14: Delete from storage bucket. If model weights encode personal data, this is a separate complex question — consult legal counsel.
column17: IMPORTANT: If your training data includes user inputs, users must have consented to this use. If they did not, audit your training dataset and rebuild without personal data or obtain retroactive consent where possible. Disclose fine-tuning in your privacy policy.

column1: Analytics Platform (Mixpanel / Amplitude / PostHog)
column3: User ID, session events, feature interactions, page views, error events, plan type, cohort membership — must NOT include prompt text or AI output
column5: Product analytics, feature adoption tracking, funnel analysis, growth metrics
column7: Collected automatically via analytics SDK during product use
column9: Mixpanel Inc. / Amplitude Inc. / PostHog Inc.
column12: SOC 2 compliant, TLS, data minimisation in event schema
column14: Delete user profile via analytics platform API or dashboard. Must be included in account deletion pipeline.
column17: Ensure prompt text and AI response content are NEVER included in analytics events. Only track behavioural metadata. Review your event schema for accidental PII leakage.

column1: Billing and Subscription Platform (Stripe / Razorpay / Chargebee)
column3: Customer name, email, billing address, payment method (tokenised), subscription plan, invoice history, payment status
column5: Subscription billing, payment processing, invoice generation
column7: Collected at first payment or subscription setup
column9: Stripe Inc. / Razorpay Financial Services / Chargebee Inc.
column12: PCI DSS compliant, TLS, tokenisation of card data
column13: Duration of subscription + 7 years (statutory financial records)
column14: Cannot fully delete — statutory financial retention applies to invoices. Anonymise non-statutory fields on account deletion.
column17: Download DPA from Stripe and Chargebee if using those platforms. Payment records are subject to statutory retention — document this exception in your inventory and inform users.

column1: Customer Support Tool (Intercom / Freshdesk)
column3: User name, email, support conversation history, product context shared in support tickets, subscription plan
column5: Customer support, bug reporting, feature requests
column14: Delete contact via platform dashboard. Include in account deletion pipeline.
column17: Ensure support tool is not used to store or replay AI output content. Keep support tickets limited to issue description and resolution notes.

column1: Email Platform (Postmark / SendGrid / AWS SES)
column3: Recipient email address, email send timestamps, delivery status, open/click events (if tracking enabled)
column5: Transactional emails — account verification, password reset, billing notifications, usage alerts
column14: Delete via platform API or log expiry.
column17: Disable email open/click tracking in transactional emails if not needed — reduces behavioural data collection. DPA available from all major providers.

column1: HR System / Payroll (internal or tool)
column3: Employee name, email, phone, PAN, bank account, salary, equity details, offer letter, performance notes
column5: Payroll, HR management, equity administration, statutory compliance
column7: Collected directly from employees during onboarding
column12: Software encryption, access restricted to Founder and HR
column14: Manual deletion after statutory period. Document deletion.
column17: Keep separate from product infrastructure. Restrict to 1–2 named individuals. Use dedicated payroll software rather than spreadsheets.

PRIORITY ACTIONS FOR AI SAAS BUSINESSES
1. Sign DPAs with every AI API provider (OpenAI / Anthropic / Google / AWS Bedrock) — non-negotiable before handling real user data
2. Verify API inputs are not used for model training by your provider — opt out if default is opt-in
3. Set inference log retention to 30–90 days maximum and implement log redaction for obvious PII
4. Include inference logs and embedding vectors in your account deletion pipeline
5. Audit training data for personal data before next fine-tuning run
6. Update privacy policy to disclose AI API providers their countries and what happens to prompt inputs
DPDP Act references:
Section 6 — Consent must disclose purpose and third-party sharing
Section 8(4) — Security safeguards for all personal data
Section 8(7) — Data must be deleted when no longer necessary
Section 11 — Access requests — must be able to retrieve all user data
Section 12 — Erasure requests — must delete including logs and vectors
Section 9 — Children's data — age gate required if product accessible to under-18s
This is not legal advice. Consult a qualified legal professional for advice specific to your organisation.
